items.question_text: What is the vertical separation minimum between IFR aircraft up to and including FL 410?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-1a: Separate IFR aircraft using 1,000 feet between altitudes up to and including FL 410.

items.question_text: What vertical separation minima applies at or above FL 290 between non-RVSM aircraft and all other aircraft at or above FL 290?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-1b: Apply 2,000 feet at or above FL 290 between non-RVSM aircraft and all other aircraft at or above FL 290.

items.question_text: What vertical separation minimum applies above FL 410 generally?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-1c: Above FL 410, the general minimum is 2,000 feet.

items.question_text: In oceanic airspace above FL 450, what separation applies between a supersonic aircraft and any other aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-1c1: In oceanic airspace above FL 450 between a supersonic and any other aircraft: 4,000 feet.

items.question_text: What vertical separation minimum applies above FL 600 between military aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-1c2: Above FL 600 between military aircraft: 5,000 feet.

items.question_text: At and below FL 410, aircraft on a magnetic course of 0 through 179 degrees should be assigned what type of altitudes?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1: 0-179 degrees = odd cardinal altitude or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals (3,000, 5,000, FL 310, FL 330, FL 350, etc.).

choices.text: Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals.

items.question_text: Above FL 410, aircraft on a magnetic course of 180 through 359 degrees should be assigned flight levels beginning with what?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1: Above FL 410, 180-359 degrees = odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL 430 (FL 430, FL 470, FL 510, etc.).

choices.text: Even cardinal flight levels beginning with FL 420.
Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL 430.
Odd cardinal flight levels beginning with FL 450.

items.question_text: For one-way routes (except composite systems), what altitude assignment rule applies below FL 410?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1: One-way routes (except composite systems): any cardinal altitude or flight level below FL 410, or any odd cardinal flight level above FL 410.

choices.text: Any cardinal altitude or flight level below FL 410, or any odd cardinal above FL 410.
Only odd altitudes regardless of direction.
Altitude assignment is at controller discretion with supervisor approval.

items.question_text: What altitude assignment rule applies to aircraft operating within an ALTRV?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1: Within an ALTRV, any course: any altitude or flight level.

choices.text: Only as specified in the ALTRV approval letter.

items.question_text: For aerial refueling tracks and anchors, how are altitudes assigned?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1: In aerial refueling tracks and anchors, altitude blocks as requested; any altitude or flight level.

choices.text: Only within published refueling anchor blocks.

items.question_text: When traffic, weather, or aircraft limitations prevent standard flight direction altitude assignment, what altitudes may be assigned per 4-5-3?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-3: Assign any cardinal altitude or flight level below FL 410 or any odd cardinal flight level at or above FL 410 without regard to direction of flight.

choices.text: Any cardinal altitude or FL below FL 410, or any odd cardinal FL at/above FL 410.
Only the next available altitude in the correct direction.
Any altitude, but the pilot must accept the deviation.

items.question_text: For traffic conditions, a non-standard altitude may be assigned if aircraft remain within the facility's area AND what condition is met?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-3a1: Must obtain prior approval from other affected positions/sectors OR operations covered in a Facility Directive.

choices.text: The pilot requests the non-standard altitude.
Prior approval from affected positions/sectors or operations covered in a Facility Directive.
The traffic condition is expected to last less than 30 minutes.

items.question_text: For aircraft operational limitations, when may a non-standard altitude be assigned without regard to flight direction?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-3d: When the pilot informs you the available appropriate altitude exceeds the aircraft's operational limitations, and only after obtaining prior approval from affected positions/sectors and, if necessary, the adjacent facility.

choices.text: Any time the pilot requests a different altitude.
Only when the pilot states the appropriate altitude exceeds aircraft limitations AND prior approval is obtained.
Automatically for any aircraft with known performance issues.
Only for military aircraft on operational missions.

items.question_text: Per TBL 4-5-2, what is the lowest usable flight level when the altimeter setting is 29.92 inches or higher?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-2: 29.92" or higher = FL 180.

items.question_text: When the altimeter setting is between 28.91 and 27.92 inches, what is the lowest usable flight level?
Per TBL 4-5-3, what adjustment factor applies when the altimeter setting is between 29.41 and 28.92 inches?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-3: 29.41" to 28.92" = 1,000 feet adjustment factor.

items.question_text: When atmospheric pressure is less than 29.92 inches and the prescribed minimum IFR altitude is at or above 18,000 feet MSL, what must be done?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-5: Add the appropriate adjustment factor from TBL 4-5-3 to the flight level equivalent of the minimum altitude to determine the adjusted minimum flight level.

choices.text: No adjustment is needed; the FL is always valid.
Add the adjustment factor from TBL 4-5-3 to the flight level equivalent.
Increase the FL by a standard 1,000 feet.
Use the next higher usable flight level from TBL 4-5-2.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft be cleared below the MEA but not below the MOCA?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-6a: Below MEA but not below MOCA if altitude is at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace AND one of: (1) within 22 miles of VOR/VORTAC/TACAN, (2) radar with MVA/MIA and lost comm instructions, or (3) GNSS equipped.

choices.text: Any time the controller deems it safe.
When altitude is at least 300 ft above controlled airspace floor AND within 22 NM of VOR, or radar with MVA/MIA, or GNSS equipped.
Only when the aircraft is in radar contact.

items.question_text: When no MCA is specified and a higher MEA applies ahead, when must the aircraft begin climb to the higher MEA?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-6c1: If no MCA is specified, prior to or immediately after passing the fix where the higher MEA is designated.

choices.text: At least 10 miles before the fix.
Prior to or immediately after passing the fix where the higher MEA is designated.
Only after passing the fix and being cleared by the next sector.

items.question_text: When an MCA is specified, when must the aircraft cross the fix?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-6c2: If an MCA is specified, prior to the fix so as to cross the fix at or above the MCA.

choices.text: Prior to the fix so as to cross at or above the MCA.
At any time as long as the altitude is reached within 5 miles.
When the pilot determines it is safe.

items.question_text: An aircraft may be cleared to operate on jet routes below the MEA under what condition?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-6b: An aircraft may be cleared on jet routes below the MEA (but not below prescribed minimum IFR altitude) or above the maximum authorized altitude if radar service is provided.

choices.text: Only with prior coordination with the receiving facility.
When the pilot accepts responsibility for terrain clearance.

items.question_text: What are GNSS MEAs and when may they be assigned?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-6d: GNSS MEAs may be approved on published ATS routes. Air traffic may assign GNSS MEAs to GNSS-equipped aircraft where established. On high altitude ATS routes, the GNSS MEA is FL 180 unless published higher.

choices.text: They are the same as conventional MEAs and apply to all aircraft.
They are MEAs assignable to GNSS-equipped aircraft where established; on high altitude routes default to FL 180.
They are optional altitudes that pilots may use at their discretion.

items.question_text: After a 'climb via' or 'descend via' clearance has been issued, if the aircraft is cleared direct to a waypoint/fix, what must be done with the clearance?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-7h4: After a 'climb via' or 'descend via' clearance has been issued, if cleared direct to a waypoint/fix, the 'climb via' or 'descend via' clearance must be restated. The SID or STAR name does not need to be restated.

choices.text: The climb via/descend via remains in effect; no restatement needed.
The 'climb via' or 'descend via' clearance must be restated.
Only the SID/STAR name needs to be restated.
A new full clearance must be issued.

items.question_text: When vectoring or approving a deviation from a climb via/descend via procedure, what must the controller state?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-7h5: State an altitude to maintain and advise the pilot if you intend on clearing the aircraft to resume the procedure. Phraseology: 'MAINTAIN (altitude), EXPECT TO RESUME STAR/SID AT (waypoint/fix).'

choices.text: Only the new heading to fly.
An altitude to maintain and, if intending to resume, advise to expect resume at a waypoint/fix.
Cancel the SID/STAR and issue a new clearance.
Tell the pilot to navigate at their own discretion.

items.question_text: A 'descend via' clearance must NOT be used when a procedure contains only what type of restrictions?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-7h6: A 'descend via' clearance must not be used where procedures contain only published 'expect' altitude and/or speed restrictions.

choices.text: Only published 'expect' altitude and/or speed restrictions.

items.question_text: What does the phraseology 'MAINTAIN (altitude)' alone do to previously issued SID/STAR altitude restrictions?
items.explanation: Per the note in 4-5-7h11: 'Maintain (altitude)' alone cancels previously issued altitude restrictions including SID/STAR restrictions unless restated or modified. Speed restrictions remain in effect unless explicitly canceled.

choices.text: It cancels altitude restrictions unless restated/modified; speed restrictions remain unless explicitly canceled.
It cancels all restrictions including speed.
It has no effect on published procedure restrictions.

items.question_text: How should a non-standard crossing altitude on a STAR be communicated?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-7h9: Emphasize the change using 'DESCEND VIA (STAR name) ARRIVAL, EXCEPT CROSS (fix), (revised altitude).'

items.question_text: When issuing a 'climb via SID except after (waypoint) maintain (altitude)' clearance, what happens after the waypoint?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-7h11 note: The aircraft should comply with all speed and altitude restrictions until reaching the waypoint and then maintain the specified altitude until cleared by ATC to continue.

choices.text: The aircraft continues climbing via the SID after the waypoint.
The aircraft maintains the specified altitude after the waypoint until cleared by ATC to continue.
The SID is canceled after the waypoint.
The pilot resumes own navigation after the waypoint.

items.question_text: An aircraft at FL 310 is flying a magnetic course of 270 degrees. Is this altitude correct per TBL 4-5-1?

choices.text: Yes, FL 310 is correct for a 270-degree course at/below FL 410.
No, a 270-degree course (180-359) at/below FL 410 requires an even altitude such as FL 320.
Yes, any altitude is valid below FL 410.
No, above FL 290 only Westbound altitudes differ.

items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1: At/below FL 410, courses 180-359 require even cardinal altitudes. FL 310 is odd and incorrect for 270 degrees; FL 320 would be correct.

items.instruction: Distinguish correct climb via / descend via phraseology.
items.question_text: An aircraft on the Eagul Five Arrival has been cleared 'descend via.' ATC needs the aircraft to cross Geeno at 10,000 instead of the published restriction. What is the correct phraseology?

choices.text: 'Descend via the Eagul Five Arrival, except cross Geeno at 10,000.'

items.explanation: Per 4-5-7h9: 'DESCEND VIA (STAR) ARRIVAL, EXCEPT CROSS (fix), (revised altitude).'
